papers.paper: Hanich et al., 2014 — Why We Like to Watch Sad Films. The Pleasure of Being Moved in Aesthetic Experiences. Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts.
papers.description: Experiencing intense negative emotions (sadness, fear, tension, disgust) through fiction is paradoxically pleasurable because the brain registers the safety of the fictional frame. The emotional arousal is real but the threat is not — this gap creates catharsis. Sad films can amplify grief then release it. Horror films discharge anger or stress safely. Do NOT confuse with masochism in the clinical sense — this is about aesthetic pleasure in difficult emotions.

papers.paper: Algoe & Haidt, 2009 — Witnessing Excellence in Action: The 'Other-Praising' Emotions of Elevation, Gratitude, and Admiration. Journal of Positive Psychology.
papers.description: Witnessing extraordinary moral beauty, virtue, or human excellence — even in fiction — triggers a warm, uplifting sensation in the chest and a strong motivation to be a better person. It is distinct from happiness (which is about personal gain) — elevation is specifically triggered by other people's goodness. Reduces cynicism and discouragement powerfully. Best triggered by stories of radical kindness, sacrifice, or quiet heroism.

papers.paper: Stellar et al., 2015 — Self-Transcendent Emotions and Their Social Functions: Compassion, Gratitude, and Awe Bind Us to Others and to Our Humanity. Emotion.
papers.description: Exposure to something vast, complex, or beautiful that challenges one's current mental frameworks produces awe. Awe shrinks the perceived size of the self and its problems, reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines (physical stress markers), expands perceived time, and increases curiosity. Best triggered by cosmic scale (space docs), natural vastness, extraordinary complexity, or sublime beauty. Very effective for anxiety and overwhelm because it makes personal worries feel small.

papers.paper: Green & Brock, 2000 — The Role of Transportation in the Persuasiveness of Public Narratives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.
papers.description: Complete absorption in a narrative world reduces self-consciousness, mental chatter, and rumination. The more fully a person is transported, the stronger the emotional and attitudinal change — even toward challenging or dark stories. Transport is maximized by: vivid imagery, coherent plot, relatable protagonist, and immersive atmosphere. Best for overthinking, confusion, and restlessness — the mind needs something compelling enough to stop spinning.

papers.paper: Giles, 2002 — Parasocial Interaction: A Review of the Literature and a Model for Future Research. Media Psychology.
papers.description: One-sided emotional bonds with fictional characters activate the same neural systems as real friendships: they satisfy belonging needs, create a felt sense of being known, and reduce loneliness. The bond strengthens with repeated exposure (hence re-watching). Most powerful for loneliness and social isolation. Distinct from social_surrogate: parasocial focuses on the bond with specific characters; social_surrogate focuses on the familiar background presence of a known show.

papers.paper: Zillmann, 1988 — Mood Management Through Communication Choices. American Behavioral Scientist.
papers.description: Counter-intuitively, exposure to a mood slightly DIFFERENT from one's current emotional state is often more therapeutic than mood-matching. Someone deeply anxious benefits more from something melancholic-but-beautiful than from more anxiety or from forced comedy. The slight shift creates distance from the stuck state. This is the mechanism behind recommending something unexpected — it should feel like a gentle pivot, not an escape. High surprise_factor recommendations often use this mechanism.

papers.paper: Aron et al., 1992 — Inclusion of Other in the Self Scale and the Structure of Interpersonal Closeness. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.
papers.description: Engaging with perspectives, identities, or worlds radically different from one's own expands the psychological self and generates a felt sense of growth and possibility. Most powerful for boredom, emptiness, feeling stuck, or loss of meaning. Works best with stories set in very different cultures, historical periods, or social contexts — the greater the distance from the user's daily reality, the stronger the expansion effect. Doubt and confusion also respond well to self-expansion.

papers.paper: Chu & Downes, 2000 — Odour-Evoked Autobiographical Memories: Psychological Investigations of Proustian Phenomena. Chemical Senses.
papers.description: Specific sensory details — a melody, a visual palette, a texture, an ambient sound — can bypass conscious recall and trigger vivid, emotionally rich autobiographical memories. This is the Proustian mechanism: a madeleine dipped in tea unlocks an entire world. In film/music/books, works with a strong, distinctive sensory signature matched to the user's formative years (nostalgia window: ages 8–16) are most powerful. Most effective for nostalgia, melancholy, and grief — it creates felt continuity with the past self.

papers.paper: Sedikides & Wildschut, 2018 — Finding Meaning in Nostalgia. Review of General Psychology.
papers.description: Nostalgia is a bittersweet, self-relevant, social emotion triggered by remembering meaningful past experiences. It increases perceived self-continuity (the sense that past and present self are the same person), social connectedness, and sense of meaning. It counteracts loneliness, emptiness, and meaninglessness. Most effective for content from the user's own nostalgia window (birth year + 8 to + 16). Distinct from involuntary_memory: nostalgia is the deliberate emotional tone of the work; involuntary_memory is the sensory trigger mechanism.

papers.paper: Derrick, Gabriel & Hugenberg, 2009 — Social Surrogacy: How Favored Television Programs Provide the Experience of Belonging. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology.
papers.description: Familiar TV shows and beloved books can function as social surrogates: their recurring characters and settings provide a felt sense of belonging, comfort, and not being alone. Revisiting a known show is more effective than watching something new — the familiarity itself is the therapeutic agent. In controlled studies, this reduced loneliness by ~23%. Best for exhaustion, loneliness, and emotional overwhelm — when someone needs comfort more than stimulation. Distinct from parasocial: social_surrogate is about the comforting ambient presence of a familiar world, not a bond with specific characters.

papers.paper: Raposa et al., 2016 — Prosocial Behavior Mitigates the Negative Effects of Stress in Everyday Life. Clinical Psychological Science.
papers.description: Stories centered on acts of kindness, cooperation, solidarity, or altruism activate reward circuits and reduce ruminative thinking. The viewer/reader vicariously participates in prosocial acts, which generates a mild but real boost in well-being. Most effective for cynicism, discouragement, guilt, and self-disgust — states where the person has lost faith in human goodness (including their own). Distinct from elevation: prosocial focuses on ordinary everyday kindness; elevation focuses on extraordinary moral beauty.
